summary:
The First Punic War (264–241 BC) was the opening conflict of the [[event:1052|Punic Wars]], fought primarily in Sicily and at sea, that ended Carthaginian control of the island and established Rome as a significant naval power for the first time. The war began with Rome's intervention in a dispute between Carthage and Syracuse over the Sicilian city of Messana — a limited intervention that spiraled into a twenty-three year struggle.

Rome's critical strategic challenge was that Carthage possessed a powerful navy and Sicily could not be conquered without controlling the surrounding sea. In an extraordinary feat of engineering and determination, Rome built a fleet of over a hundred warships — allegedly using a beached Carthaginian ship as the template — within sixty days, and trained crews on rowers drilling on land before the ships were even launched. The Romans compensated for their sailors' inexperience by inventing the corvus, a spiked boarding bridge that turned sea battles into the land fighting at which they excelled. An early naval victory at the [[event:1060|Battle of Mylae]] demonstrated this approach's effectiveness.

The war saw enormous losses on both sides, largely from storms rather than battle — Roman fleets of hundreds of ships were destroyed multiple times by storms. Roman resilience was remarkable: each time, Rome rebuilt its fleet and continued fighting. The war ended when a final Roman naval victory at the Aegates Islands in 241 BC cut off Carthage's forces in Sicily, forcing a negotiated peace. Carthage ceded Sicily and paid a large indemnity; Sicily became Rome's first overseas province, providing the administrative model that Rome would replicate across the Mediterranean.
